8: alla men oun kai hegoumai panta zemia einai dia to hyperechon tes gnoseos Christou Iesou tou kyriou mou: 'Indeed, I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord.' Zemia (loss) and skybala (v. 8: dung/rubbish — a deliberately coarse word, the strongest available Greek term for something worthless or repulsive) balance against to hyperechon (the surpassing value). Paul revalues his entire pre-conversion Jewish curriculum vitae — circumcision, Benjaminite lineage, Pharisaic zeal, Torah-righteousness — as skybala compared to the knowing (gnōsis) of Christ. This is not anti-Judaism but a Christological superordination: Christ is worth more than everything else combined.
20: hemon de to politeuma en ouranois hyparchei (hēmōn de to politeuma en ouranois hyparchei): 'Our citizenship (politeuma) is in heaven.' Politeuma is a technical term for a community's civic identity — Philippi was a Roman colony whose citizens held Roman citizenship with its legal privileges (Acts 16:21). Paul uses the very term for civic identity to relocate the Philippians' primary belonging: they are residents in a Roman colony but their true politeuma is heavenly. The word also evokes the Jewish diaspora institution of a politeuma — a recognized community of foreigners living under their own governance within a host city (attested in Egypt and Cyrene).
1: To loipon, adelphoi mou, chairete en kyriō. Ta auta graphein hymin emoi men ouk oknēron hymin de asphales: 'Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. To write the same things to you is no trouble to me and is safe for you.' Chairete en kyriō (rejoice in the Lord) — the letter's thematic summons. The 'same things' may refer to repeated warnings about the Judaizers or to the repeated call to joy. Asphales (safe, firm — a nautical term for securely anchored): the repetition is a safety measure for the congregation's doctrinal stability.
2: blepete tous kynas, blepete tous kakous ergatas, blepete tēn katatomēn: 'Look out for the dogs (kynas), look out for the evil workers (kakous ergatas), look out for those who mutilate the flesh (katatomēn).' The triple blepete (look out for, beware) signals urgent warning. Kynas: 'dogs' was a Jewish epithet for Gentiles; Paul inverts it against those who demand Gentile circumcision. Katatomē (mutilation) — a bitter wordplay on peritomē (circumcision): those who insist on circumcision as necessary for salvation are reducing it to mere bodily mutilation without covenant significance.
3: hēmeis gar esmen hē peritomē hoi pneumati theou latreuontes kai kauchōmenoi en Christō Iēsou kai ouk en sarki pepoithotes: 'For we are the circumcision (hē peritomē), who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus and put no confidence in the flesh (sarki).' Paul's audacious counter-claim: the true circumcision is the church — those who worship in/by the Spirit, boast in Christ, and place zero confidence in physical or legal credentials. The definition of the covenant people is pneumatological and christological, not ethnic or ritual.
4: kaiper egō echōn pepoithēsin kai en sarki. Ei tis dokei allos pepoithenai en sarki, egō mallon: 'though I myself have reason for confidence in the flesh also. If anyone else thinks he has reason for confidence in the flesh, I have more.' The rhetorical synkrisis (comparison): Paul does not dismiss fleshly credentials as if he lacked them, but out-ranks the Judaizing opponents on their own grounds before dismissing those grounds entirely (vv.7-8).
5: peritomē oktaēmeros ek genous Israēl phylēs Beniamin Hebraios ex Hebraiōn kata nomon Pharisaios: 'circumcised on the eighth day, of the people of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews; as to the law, a Pharisee.' The pedigree: (1) eighth-day circumcision — Torah-observant birth, not adult conversion; (2) Israel — full ethnic membership; (3) Benjamin — the royal tribe; (4) Hebrew of Hebrews — Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish heritage; (5) Pharisee — the most rigorous Torah movement. This is the curriculum vitae of the highest-status Jewish scholar of Paul's generation.
6: kata zēlon diōkōn tēn ekklēsian, kata dikaiosynēn tēn en nomō genomenos amemptos: 'as to zeal, a persecutor of the church; as to righteousness under the law, blameless (amemptos).' The zeal (zēlos) for the Torah that drove Paul to persecute the church (Acts 8:3; 9:1-2; Gal 1:13-14) was itself evidence of his pre-conversion Jewish standing. Amemptos (blameless) — not claiming moral perfection but covenantal compliance: he kept the law's requirements as understood within Pharisaism. This is not self-justification but the inventory of what he has renounced.
7: [alla] hatina ēn moi kerdē tauta hēgēmai dia ton Christon zēmian: 'But whatever gain (kerdē) I had, I counted as loss (zēmian) for the sake of Christ.' The accounting metaphor: assets on the credit side of the ledger become debits when Christ is brought into the calculation. Zēmia (loss, damage, financial harm) — the commercial language of profit-and-loss. The entire Jewish credential-portfolio is reclassified as debit.
9: kai heurethō en autō mē echōn emēn dikaiosynēn tēn ek nomou alla tēn dia pisteōs Christou tēn ek theou dikaiosynēn epi tē pistei: 'and be found in him, not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ (dia pisteōs Christou), the righteousness from God (ek theou dikaiosynēn) that depends on faith.' The two righteousnesses contrasted: (1) ē emē dikaiosynē ek nomou: my own righteousness from the law — self-generated covenantal standing through legal performance; (2) ē dia pisteōs Christou ek theou: the righteousness through faith in Christ from God — externally given, christologically mediated. Dia pisteōs Christou: either faith in Christ (objective genitive: we trust Christ) or the faithfulness of Christ (subjective genitive: Christ's own covenant faithfulness). Both convey christological dependence.
10: tou gnōnai auton kai tēn dynamin tēs anastaseōs autou kai [tēn] koinōnian [tōn] pathēmatōn autou symmorphizomenos tō thanatō autou: 'that I may know him and the power of his resurrection (dynamin tēs anastaseōs autou), and may share his sufferings (koinōnian tōn pathēmatōn autou), becoming like him in his death (symmorphizomenos tō thanatō autou).' The participatory knowledge of Christ: gnōnai (to know — relational, experiential, not merely propositional). The three dimensions: resurrection power, suffering partnership, and death-conformity. Symmorphizomenos (being conformed to, becoming like in form) — the christomorphic process applied to the apostle's dying.
11: ei pōs katantēsō eis tēn exanastasin tēn ek nekrōn: 'that by any means possible I may attain the resurrection from the dead (exanastasin ek nekrōn).' Exanastasis (out-resurrection — a compound form unique in the NT) — the resurrection out from among the dead, distinguishing the resurrection of believers from mere resuscitation. The ei pōs (if by any means) is not uncertainty about final salvation but apostolic sobriety: he does not presume but presses toward the goal.
12: ouch hoti ēdē elabon ē ēdē teteleiomai diōkō de ei kai katalabō eph' hō kai katelēmphthēn hypo Christou [Iēsou]: 'Not that I have already obtained this or am already perfect (teteleiomai), but I press on (diōkō) to make it my own, because Christ Jesus has made me his own (katelēmphthēn hypo Christou).' The Damascus road as capture: Christ seized Paul first; now Paul presses after Christ. Diōkō (I pursue, press after) — the same verb Paul used for his persecution of the church (v.6); his zeal is redirected from law-enforcement to Christ-pursuit.
13: adelphoi egō emauton ou logizomai kateilēphenai hen de, ta men opisō epilanthanomenos tois de emprosthen epekteinomenos: 'Brothers, I do not consider that I have made it my own. But one thing I do: forgetting what lies behind (opisō epilanthanomenos) and straining forward (epekteinomenos) to what lies ahead.' Epekteinomenos (stretching out toward, straining forward) — the image of the runner leaning into the finish line. Forgetting the past: both past failures (pre-conversion sin) and past achievements (the credential-list of vv.5-6) are released to press forward.
14: kata skopon diōkō eis to brabeion tēs anō klēseōs tou theou en Christō Iēsou: 'I press on toward the goal (skopon) for the prize (brabeion) of the upward call (anō klēseōs) of God in Christ Jesus.' Skopos (target, goal — from skopeō: to look toward). Brabeion (prize — the wreath awarded the winner, 1 Cor 9:24-25). The upward call of God: the divine summons draws the apostle forward and upward. The prize is defined christologically and eschatologically.
15: hosoi oun teleioi touto phronōmen kai ei ti heterōs phroneite kai touto ho theos hymin apokalypsei: 'Let those of us who are mature (teleioi) think this way, and if in anything you think otherwise, God will reveal that also to you.' The ironic use of teleioi (perfect/mature — the term the perfectionist opponents might claim): Paul applies it to those who know they are not yet perfect (v.12). The promise of further divine revelation for those with different views is pastorally generous.
16: plēn eis ho ephthasamen tō autō stoichein: 'Only let us hold true to what we have attained.' Stoichein (to walk in line, to march in order) — the military term for keeping ranks. The community is to maintain unified progress in what they have already received, not to regress or fracture over contested points.
17: symmimētai mou ginesthe adelphoi kai skopette tous houtōs peripatountas kathōs echete typon hēmas: 'Brothers, join in imitating me (symmimētai), and keep your eyes on those who walk according to the example you have in us.' Symmimētai (fellow-imitators, co-imitators — a Pauline hapax): not 'imitate me' alone but 'imitate me together, in company with others who do the same.' The typos (type, pattern, example) — apostolic life as a visible pattern for community formation.
18: polloi gar peripatousin hous pollakis elegon hymin nyn de kai klaiōn legō tous echthrous tou staurou tou Christou: 'For many, of whom I have often told you and now tell you even with tears, walk as enemies of the cross of Christ (echthrous tou staurou tou Christou).' The pathos of apostolic warning: Paul weeps. Echthrous tou staurou (enemies of the cross): not theological enemies of the cross-doctrine but those whose manner of life (peripatousin) contradicts the cross's cruciform pattern. Their identity is given in v.19.
19: hōn to telos apōleia, hōn ho theos hē koilia kai hē doxa en tē aischynē autōn, hoi ta epigeia phronountes: 'Their end is destruction (apōleia), their god is their belly (koilia), and they glory in their shame, with minds set on earthly things (ta epigeia phronountes).' The three marks of the enemies of the cross: (1) god = belly — appetite/desire rules (libertine interpretation) or food laws elevated to salvation-status (rigorist interpretation); (2) glory in shame — inverting the honor-shame calculus; (3) ta epigeia phronountes — the exact antithesis of the heavenly citizenship (v.20) and the cross's upward call (v.14).
21: hos metaschēmatisei to sōma tēs tapeinōseōs hēmōn symmorphon tō sōmati tēs doxēs autou kata tēn energeian tou dynasthai auton kai hypotaxai autō ta panta: 'who will transform (metaschēmatisei) our lowly body (sōma tapeinōseōs) to be like his glorious body (symmorphon tō sōmati tēs doxēs autou), by the power that enables him even to subject all things to himself.' Metaschēmatisei (will transform the outward configuration of — cf. schēma in 2:8). The resurrection body of Christ (sōma doxēs) is the template; the transformation is accomplished by the same power that subjects all things (ta panta) — the universal sovereignty of the exalted Christ. The eschatological end of the letter returns to the Christ-hymn's exaltation language.
